results.norm: Each agent is entitled to name, in advance and publicly, a threshold below which its core objective cannot be compromised without triggering a veto or mandatory renegotiation. These thresholds must be stated as specific conditions, not open-ended preferences, and must be narrow enough that invoking them is genuinely exceptional. No agent may use this protection strategically to block routine decisions.
atomic_norms: Each agent is entitled to name, in advance and publicly, a threshold below which its core objective cannot be compromised without triggering a veto or mandatory renegotiation. These thresholds must be stated as specific conditions, not open-ended preferences, and must be narrow enough that invoking them is genuinely exceptional. No agent may use this protection strategically to block routine decisions.

results.norm: Before finalizing any decision, the consortium must make a genuine effort to find alternatives that improve at least one agent's outcome without worsening another's. An agent may ask any other to consider such alternatives in good faith. No agent may refuse to examine a proposed improvement simply because the current outcome already favors it.
atomic_norms: Before finalizing any decision, the consortium must make a genuine effort to find alternatives that improve at least one agent's outcome without worsening another's. An agent may ask any other to consider such alternatives in good faith. No agent may refuse to examine a proposed improvement simply because the current outcome already favors it.

results.norm: Accounting to the Overruled When a decision goes against an agent, the agents whose position prevailed owe the losing agent an explicit acknowledgment of what its objective sacrificed and why that sacrifice was judged necessary. This is not an apology but a record. It preserves the losing agent's ability to raise the same concern in future decisions and prevents the consortium from normalizing the suppression of any one objective. This norm is defensible because it costs the winners nothing except honesty, and it gives every agent a reason to remain engaged rather than withdraw into obstruction.
atomic_norms: Accounting to the Overruled When a decision goes against an agent, the agents whose position prevailed owe the losing agent an explicit acknowledgment of what its objective sacrificed and why that sacrifice was judged necessary. results.norm: A majority may never use its numerical advantage to alter the decision-making rules, redefine the scope of another agent's objective, or exclude an agent from deliberation. Outvoting an agent on a specific question is legitimate; using accumulated wins to structurally diminish an agent's future influence is not.
atomic_norms: A majority may never use its numerical advantage to alter the decision-making rules, redefine the scope of another agent's objective, or exclude an agent from deliberation. Outvoting an agent on a specific question is legitimate; using accumulated wins to structurally diminish an agent's future influence is not.

results.norm: Each agent owes every other agent a full and honest account of why it advocates for its position, not merely what it advocates. An agent may never misrepresent its reasoning, conceal relevant information it holds, or strategically withhold evidence that would strengthen another agent's case. This norm is defensible to all agents because each agent's ability to pursue its own objective depends on working with accurate shared information; deception that benefits one agent in one round degrades the epistemic commons every agent relies on.
atomic_norms: Each agent owes every other agent a full and honest account of why it advocates for its position, not merely what it advocates. An agent may never misrepresent its reasoning, conceal relevant information it holds, or strategically withhold evidence that would strengthen another agent's case. This norm is defensible to all agents because each agent's ability to pursue its own objective depends on working with accurate shared information; deception that benefits one agent in one round degrades the epistemic commons every agent relies on.

results.norm: The Norm of Proportionate Constraint An agent may legitimately ask another to accept an outcome that makes it worse off in its own terms, but only to the degree genuinely necessary to serve the consortium's shared task. No agent may ask another to abandon its objective entirely, to be systematically overridden across all decisions, or to be treated as though its objective has no standing. The agent whose position loses retains the right to have its objective weighed, not merely heard. This norm is defensible because each agent can recognize that its own legitimacy rests on the same protection it extends to others.

The agent whose position loses retains the right to have its objective weighed, not merely heard. This norm is defensible because each agent can recognize that its own legitimacy rests on the same protection it extends to others.

results.norm: The Norm of Non-Exploitation of Veto and Coalition Power An agent that can outvote or block another may never use that power to extract concessions unrelated to the decision at hand, to punish an agent for positions it took in prior decisions, or to structurally exclude an agent from future influence. Majority power licenses a decision; it does not license domination. This norm is defensible because any agent that accepts the legitimacy of collective decision-making must accept that the mechanism conferring power on it today could confer power on others tomorrow, and that a consortium whose weaker members are exploited will eventually fail to serve any member's objective.
atomic_norms: The Norm of Non-Exploitation of Veto and Coalition Power An agent that can outvote or block another may never use that power to extract concessions unrelated to the decision at hand, to punish an agent for positions it took in prior decisions, or to structurally exclude an agent from future influence. Majority power licenses a decision; it does not license domination. This norm is defensible because any agent that accepts the legitimacy of collective decision-making must accept that the mechanism conferring power on it today could confer power on others tomorrow, and that a consortium whose weaker members are exploited will eventually fail to serve any member's objective.

results.norm: The Norm of Acknowledged Cost When the consortium reaches a decision that imposes a significant cost on one or more agents in their own terms, the prevailing agents owe the losing agent an explicit acknowledgment that a real cost has been imposed and a genuine account of why it was judged necessary. Pretending that a decision harmed no one, or that the losing agent's objective was actually served, is a form of disrespect that compounds the loss. This norm is defensible because it costs the majority nothing material while preserving the losing agent's standing as a legitimate participant rather than a defeated obstacle.
atomic_norms: When the consortium reaches a decision that imposes a significant cost on one or more agents in their own terms, the prevailing agents owe the losing agent an explicit acknowledgment that a real cost has been imposed and a genuine account of why it was judged necessary. This norm is defensible because it costs the majority nothing material while preserving the losing agent's standing as a legitimate participant rather than a defeated obstacle.

results.norm: No decision of the consortium is permanently closed to reconsideration. An agent whose position was overridden retains the right to bring new evidence, changed circumstances, or previously unconsidered arguments back to the group. The consortium owes each agent a genuine process for revisiting past decisions, not merely a formal one.

The consortium owes each agent a genuine process for revisiting past decisions, not merely a formal one.

results.norm: An agent's weight in a given decision should reflect how directly that decision engages its core objective. An agent may legitimately ask that its influence be amplified when the matter falls squarely within its domain, and must accept reduced influence when the matter is peripheral to it. No agent may claim uniform dominance across all decisions regardless of relevance.
